content: Which one of the following figures represents the radial electric field distribution $E_R$ caused by a spherical cloud of electrons with a volume charge density. $\rho=-3 \rho_0$ for $0 \leq R \leq a$ (both $\rho_0, a$ are positive and $R$ is the radial distance) and $\rho=0$ for $R>a$ ?

options.content: Fig. (i)
Fig. (ii)
Fig. (iii)
Fig. (iv)

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { }\\ Given:\,\,\,\, &\rho_V=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -3 \rho_0 c / m^3 & 0 \leq R \leq a \\ 0 & R>a \end{array}\right. \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Case (i) : } R \leq a \text { : }\\ &\begin{aligned} \oint \bar{D} \cdot d \bar{S} & =Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\int \rho_V d V \\ Q_{\mathrm{enc}} & =\int \rho_V d V=-\int 3 \rho_0 R^2 \sin \theta d \theta d \phi d R \\ \Rightarrow \quad Q_{\mathrm{enc}} & =-3 \rho_0 \int_{R=0}^R R^2 d R \int_{\theta=0}^\pi \sin \theta d \theta \int_{\phi=0}^{2 \pi} d \phi \\ \Rightarrow \quad Q_{\mathrm{enc}} & =-\left.3 \rho_0 \cdot \frac{R^3}{3}\right|_0 ^R-\left.\left.\cos \theta\right|_0 ^\pi \cdot \phi\right|_0 ^{2 \pi} \\ \Rightarrow \quad Q_{\mathrm{enc}} & =-\rho_0 \cdot a^3 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \pi=-\left(4 \pi R^3\right) \rho_0 \\ \oint \bar{D} \cdot d \bar{S} & =D \times 4 \pi R^2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} = & D .4 \pi R^2=-4 \pi R^3 \rho_0 \\ \Rightarrow & D=-\rho_0 R \\ \Rightarrow & E=-\frac{\rho_0 R}{\epsilon_0} \\ \Rightarrow & \bar{E}=-\frac{\rho_0 R}{\epsilon_0} \hat{a}_r \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Case (ii) : } R>a\\ &\begin{aligned} \oint \bar{D} \cdot d \bar{S} & =Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\int \rho_V d v \\ Q_{\mathrm{enc}} & =\int-3 \rho_0 \cdot R^2 \sin \theta d \theta d \phi d R \\ \Rightarrow \quad Q_{\mathrm{enc}} & =-3 \rho_0 \int_{R=0}^a R^2 d R \int_{\theta=0}^\pi \sin \theta d \theta \int_{\phi=0}^{2 \pi} d \phi \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad Q_{\mathrm{enc}} & =-\left.3 \rho_0 \cdot \frac{R^3}{3}\right|_0 ^a-\left.\left.\cos \theta\right|_0 ^\pi \cdot \phi\right|_0 ^{2 \pi}=-\rho_0 a^3 \pi \cdot 2 \pi=-4 \pi a^3 \rho_0 \\ \oint \bar{D} \cdot d \bar{S} & =D \times 4 \pi R^2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{lr} \therefore & D .4 \pi R^2=-4 \pi a^3 \rho_0 \\ \Rightarrow & D=-\frac{\rho_0 a^3}{R^2} \\ \Rightarrow & E=-\frac{\rho_0 a^3}{\epsilon R^2} \\ \Rightarrow & \bar{E}=\frac{-\rho_0 a^3}{\epsilon_0 R^2} \hat{a}_R \end{array} $$

$$ Hence,\bar{E}=\left\{\begin{array}{lc} \frac{-\rho_0 R}{\epsilon_0} \hat{a}_r, & 0 \leq R \leq a \\ \frac{-\rho_0 a^3}{\epsilon_0 R^2} \hat{a}_r, & R>a \end{array}\right. $$

Graph :

content:
A $1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ point charge is held at the origin of a cartesian coordinate system. If a second point charge of $10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is moved from $(0,10,0)$ to $(5,5,5)$ and subsequently to $(5,0,0)$, then the total work done is

$\_\_\_\_$ mJ . (Round off to 2 decimal places). Take $\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9$ in SI units. All coordinates are in meters.
explanation:
Given :

Now electric field because of $1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \times r^2} \hat{a}_r=\vec{E}_r \\ & \vec{\nabla} \times \vec{E}_r=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{a}_r & \hat{a}_\theta & a_\phi \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial_r} & \frac{\partial}{\partial_\theta} & \frac{\partial}{\partial_\phi} \\ F(r) & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right| \end{aligned} $$

$\vec{\nabla} \times \vec{E}=0$ as electric field is curl free, path independent, irrotational. As electric field is path independent so instead of going from A to B to C . We can go from A to C directly.

$$ \begin{aligned}So,\,\, & V_{A C}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 10}-\frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 5} \\ & V_{A C}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left\{\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{5}\right\}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{1}{10} \end{aligned} $$

So now work done $=q V$

$$ \begin{aligned} & W=10 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \times 10} \\ & W=10 \times 10^{-12} \times 9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

Work done $=0.009$ Joules $=9 \mathrm{~mJ}$

Hence, the correct answer is 9 .

will collide first?

An air filled cylindrical capacitor (capacitance $C_0$ ) of length $L$, with $a$ and $b$ as its inner and outer radii, respectively, consists of two coaxial conducting surfaces. Its crosssectional view is shown in Figure (i). In order to increase the capacitance, a dielectric material of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ is inserted inside $50 \%$ of the annular region as shown in figure (ii). The value of $\varepsilon_r$ for which the capacitance of the capacitor in figure (ii), becomes $5 C_0$ is
explanation:
Capacitance, $\quad C_0=\frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 l}{\ln \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)}$

Now, capacitance, $\quad C_{\text {eq }}=C_1+C_2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & C_1 & =\frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 l}{\ln \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\pi \varepsilon_0 l}{\ln \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)} \\ & C_2 & =\frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r l}{\ln \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r l}{\ln \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)} \\ \therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,& C_{\text {eq }} & =\frac{\pi \varepsilon_0 l}{\ln \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)}\left[1+\varepsilon_r\right] \end{aligned} $$

According to question,

$$ C_{\text {eq }}=5 C_0 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\pi \varepsilon_0 l}{\ln \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)}\left[1+\varepsilon_r\right] & =5 \times \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 l}{\ln \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)} \\ 1+\varepsilon_r & =10 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\varepsilon_r=9 $$

content: In the $(x, y, z)$ coordinate system, three point-charges $Q$, $Q$, and $\alpha Q$ are located in free space at $(-1, 0, 0)$, $(1, 0, 0)$, and $(0, -1, 0)$, respectively. The value of $\alpha$ for the electric field to be zero at $(0, 0.5, 0)$ is _________________ (rounded off to 1 decimal place).

The closed curve shown in the figure is described by

$$r = 1 + \cos \theta $$, where $$r = \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} ;x = r\cos \theta ,y = r\sin \theta $$

The magnitude of the line integral of the vector field $$F = - y\widehat i + x\widehat j$$ around the closed curve is ___________ (Round off to 2 decimal places).
explanation:
Given vector is, $\vec{F}=-y \hat{i}+x \hat{j}$

$$ \oint \vec{F} d \vec{l}=\oint(-y) d x+(x) d y $$

$\begin{aligned} & \left.\oint \vec{F} d \vec{l}=\iint 1-(-1) d x d y=2 \iint d x d y=2 \text { (Area of the cardioid } r=1+\cos \theta\right)=2\left\{\int_0^{2 \pi} \int_0^{1+\cos \theta} d r d \theta\right\} \\\\ & =2\left\{\int_0^{2 \pi} \frac{(1+\cos \theta)^2}{2} d \theta\right\}=\left\{\int_0^{2 \pi}\left(2 \cos ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}\right) d \theta\right\} \\\\ & =\int_0^{2 \pi}\left(4 \cos ^4 \frac{\theta}{2}\right) d \theta \\ & \end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\theta}{2}=t \\\\ & \frac{d \theta}{2}=d t, \text { for } \theta=2 \pi \quad t=\pi, \text { for } \theta=0 \quad t=0 \\\\ & =\int_0^\pi 4 \cdot \cos ^4 t 2 d t=8 \int_0^\pi \cos ^4 t d t \\\\ & =8(2) \int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos ^4 t d t=16\left(\frac{3.1}{4.2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}\right)=3 \pi=9.42\end{aligned}$

content:
A quadratic function of two variables is given as

$$f({x_1},{x_2}) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 + 3{x_1} + 3{x_2} + {x_1}{x_2} + 1$$

The magnitude of the maximum rate of change of the function at the point (1, 1) is ___________ (Round off to the nearest integer).
explanation:
To find the magnitude of the maximum rate of change of the given function at the point $(1, 1)$, we need to compute the gradient of the function. The gradient vector represents the direction and rate of the steepest ascent.

The given quadratic function is:

$$f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 + 3x_1 + 3x_2 + x_1 x_2 + 1$$

The gradient vector $\nabla f$ is calculated by finding the partial derivatives of $f$ with respect to $x_1$ and $x_2$.

First, compute the partial derivative with respect to $x_1$:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_1} \left( x_1^2 + 2 x_2^2 + 3 x_1 + 3 x_2 + x_1 x_2 + 1 \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2 x_1 + 3 + x_2$$

Next, compute the partial derivative with respect to $x_2$:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_2} \left( x_1^2 + 2 x_2^2 + 3 x_1 + 3 x_2 + x_1 x_2 + 1 \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 4 x_2 + 3 + x_1$$

Now, evaluate the gradient vector at the point $(1, 1)$:

$$\left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \right|_{(1,1)} = 2(1) + 3 + 1 = 6$$

$$\left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \right|_{(1,1)} = 4(1) + 3 + 1 = 8$$

So, the gradient vector at point $(1, 1)$ is:

$$\nabla f(1, 1) = (6, 8)$$

The magnitude of the gradient vector is given by the Euclidean norm:

$$|\nabla f(1, 1)| = \sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10$$

Therefore, the magnitude of the maximum rate of change of the function at the point $(1, 1)$ is 10.

content: As shown in the figure below, to concentric conducting spherical shells, centred at r = 0 and having radii r = c and r = d are maintained at potentials such that the potential V(r) at r = c is V1 and V(r) at r = d is V2. Assume that V(r) depends only on r, where r is the radial distance. The expression for V(r) in the region between r = c and r = d is
explanation:
From Laplace's equation,

$${\nabla ^2}V = 0$$

For spherical co-ordinate system,

$$\matrix{ {{h_1}} & {{h_2}} & {{h_3}} \cr 1 & r & {r\sin \theta } \cr } $$

$$\therefore$$ $${\nabla ^2}V = {1 \over {{r^2}\sin \theta }}\left[ {{\partial \over {\partial r}}\left( {{{{r^2}\sin \theta } \over 1}\,.\,{{\partial V} \over {\partial r}}} \right)} \right] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {r^2}{{dV} \over {dr}} = A$$; A = Constant

$$ \Rightarrow {{dV} \over {dr}} = {A \over {{r^2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow V = {{ - A} \over r} + B$$; B = Constant

at r = c; $$V = {V_1}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {V_1} = {{ - A} \over c} + B$$ ...... (1)

at r = d; $$V = {V_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {V_2} = {{ - A} \over d} + B$$ ...... (2)

(1) $$-$$ (2), we get

$${V_1} - {V_2} = {{ - A} \over c} + {A \over d}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {V_1} - {V_2} = A\left[ {{{c - d} \over {cd}}} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow A = \left( {{{{V_1} - {V_2}} \over {c - d}}} \right)cd$$

From (1),

$$B = {V_1} + {A \over c}$$

$$ \Rightarrow B = {V_1} + \left( {{{{V_1} - {V_2}} \over {c - d}}} \right)d$$

$$\therefore$$ $$V = {{ - \left( {{{{V_1} - {V_2}} \over {c - d}}} \right)cd} \over r} + {V_1} + d\left( {{{{V_1} - {V_2}} \over {c - d}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow V(r) = {{({V_1} - {V_2})cd} \over {(d - c)r}} + {V_1} - d\left( {{{{V_1} - {V_2}} \over {d - c}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow V(r) = {{({V_1} - {V_2})cd} \over {(d - c)r}} + {{{V_1}d - {V_1}c - {V_1}d + {V_2}d} \over {d - c}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow V(r) = {{cd({V_1} - {V_2})} \over {(d - c)r}} + {{{V_2}d - {V_1}c} \over {d - c}}$$

content: Consider a large parallel plate capacitor. The gap $d$ between the two plates is filled entirely with a dielectric slab of relative permittivity 5 . The plates are initially charged to a potential difference of V volts and then disconnected from the source. If the dielectric slab is pulled out completely, then the ratio of the new electric field $E_2$ in the gap to the original electric field $E_1$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
explanation:
Large parallel plate capacitor indicates, fringing effect can be neglected and had $E$-field is same as infinite surface charge.

Initial case :

$$ \begin{aligned} & E_{\text {old }}=\frac{V}{l}=\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0 \times 5} \\ & \rho_{s_{\text {old }}}=\frac{V}{l} \times 5 \varepsilon_0=E_{\text {old }} \times 5 \varepsilon_0 \end{aligned} $$

After disconnection charge will be conserved.

$$ \begin{aligned} & E_{\text {new }}=\frac{\rho_{s_{\text {old }}}}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{\frac{V}{l} \times 5 \varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon_0} \\ & E_{\text {new }}=5 E_{\text {old }} \\ & \frac{E_2}{E_1}=5 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 5 .
